title: Wax emulsion and salt resist on clay

glossaryTerms.definition: fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
clay diluted with water to a brushable or pourable consistency
the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join

content.text: Resist decoration uses a substance that repels liquid colour to create pattern. This tutorial covers two resist methods for air-dry clay. The first uses wax emulsion brushed onto a surface before a coloured
slip

is applied: the waxed areas repel the slip and remain light against a coloured ground. The second uses coarse salt sprinkled onto wet slip: as the salt crystals absorb moisture from the slip they create a crystalline, pitted texture. Both techniques work on flat slabs at the
bone-dry
stage and can be combined in sequence on the same piece.
When to use it
Both methods require the clay to be bone-dry before any resist is applied. Applying wax or salt to damp or
leather-hard

clay risks softening the surface or causing the base to crack when additional moisture from the slip is introduced. Use these techniques on flat slab tiles, shallow dishes, or decorative plaques where the surface is accessible and horizontal.
What you need
Step by step
Confirm the clay is bone-dry. Set it on the wooden board on a level surface.

Wax method: load the liner brush with undiluted wax emulsion and paint your design directly onto the bone-dry clay surface. The wax dries to a pale, slightly glossy film in about five minutes. Do not rush this step: if the wax is still wet when you apply slip, it will lift rather than resist.

Once the wax is dry to the touch, brush the coloured slip over the entire surface with the mop brush in a single smooth pass. The slip will bead on the wax areas and pool away from them, while sinking into the unwaxed clay.
Leave the slip to dry until it is no longer wet to the touch, about 20 to 30 minutes. The wax areas will remain lighter and slightly raised. Do not wipe or sponge at this stage.

Salt method (alternative or additional layer): instead of or after the wax step, brush a fresh coat of coloured slip over the surface. Immediately sprinkle coarse sea salt crystals onto the wet slip : one crystal every 5 to 10 mm. The salt draws moisture from the slip as it dissolves, leaving a starburst of lighter, crystalline texture around each crystal.

Leave the salt in place until the slip is completely dry, at least two hours. Do not touch the surface while the salt is working. Once dry, brush away the salt crystals with a dry, soft brush. The pitted impressions beneath will remain.
When all layers are dry, seal the entire piece with two coats of undiluted PVA applied with a clean mop brush. Allow each coat to dry clear before adding the next.
What it looks like when it works

The wax-resist areas show as clean, light-coloured shapes against a solid coloured ground. The edges between waxed and unwaxed areas are slightly soft rather than perfectly hard: this is normal and gives the technique a painted quality. Salt-resist areas show as irregular, radiating halos of lighter colour, each surrounding the site of a dissolved crystal. Combined, the two methods produce a surface that reads as both controlled (the wax pattern) and organic (the salt texture).
Common mistakes
Wax applied to damp clay: the emulsion does not dry properly and the slip dissolves the wax film rather than beading on it. Start only from bone-dry.
Slip brushed over wet wax: the wax smears and the resist breaks down. Wait until the wax film is dry to the touch before flooding with slip.
Salt crystals too dense: the starbursts overlap and merge into a single pale field rather than individual marks. Reduce spacing to give each crystal room to work.
Removing salt before slip is dry: lifting the crystals too soon drags the wet slip with them and leaves smear marks. The slip needs to be fully dry before brushing salt away.
PVA applied to uncleaned salt residue: a fine white crust under the PVA makes the seal look milky. Brush all salt off thoroughly with a dry brush before sealing.
